question: [ACX 2026] Will the winner of the 2026 FIFA World Cup be a country that has never won before?
resolution_criterion:
(https://www.metaculus.com/questions/embed/41090)
Resolves according to Metaculus resolution.

Metaculus high-level description:
This question will resolve as Yes if the winner of 2026 FIFA World Cup is a country other than Uruguay, Italy, Germany, Brazil, England, Argentina, France, or Spain.
reference_class: World Cup tournaments where all remaining semifinalists are past champions
reasoning: Research (not pure forecasting) resolves this almost entirely: as of 2026-07-12 the World Cup has reached the semifinal stage with exactly four teams remaining — France, Spain, England, and Argentina (quarterfinal results: France 2-0 Morocco, Spain 2-1 Belgium, England 2-1 Norway, Argentina 3-1 Switzerland). All four are explicitly named on the resolution criteria's list of prior winners that would make this resolve NO. Since the winner must come from this pool of four, a first-time winner is impossible under normal completion of the tournament. Residual probability only reflects extreme tail risk (tournament voided/abandoned, mass disqualification, or some technicality) rather than genuine competitive uncertainty.
what_would_change_my_mind: Tournament suspension, abandonment, or a semifinal/final forfeiture/disqualification before completion
Any credible report that the field has changed from France/Spain/England/Argentina

question: Will a previous winner of the World Cup win the 2026 FIFA World Cup?
resolution_criterion: Resolves YES if the winner of 2026 FIFA World Cup is Brazil, Germany, Italy, Argentina, France, Uruguay, Spain, or England.
reference_class: Prior FIFA World Cup tournaments (1930-2022) by which nations have won the title
reasoning: The 2026 World Cup semifinals are set: France vs Spain (July 14, Dallas) and England vs Argentina (July 15, Atlanta), with the final on July 19 at MetLife Stadium. All four remaining teams are on the YES resolution list (previous World Cup winners), while non-qualifying teams (Germany, Brazil, Uruguay, Belgium, Morocco, Norway, Switzerland, Morocco again, etc.) have already been eliminated. Historically, these 8 nations have won 100% of all 22 prior World Cups, so the base rate was already extremely high; the actual bracket outcome now makes it a near-certainty rather than just likely. Residual uncertainty is limited to tail-risk scenarios: tournament disruption/cancellation before July 19, or a resolution technicality — none of which have any signal behind them.
what_would_change_my_mind: News of the July 19 final being postponed, cancelled, or abandoned
Any credible report contradicting the current semifinal lineup (France/Spain, England/Argentina)
A dispute or ambiguity in how Manifold's market creator interprets 'winner' if the final can't be completed

question: Will a previous winner of the World Cup win the 2026 FIFA World Cup?
resolution_criterion: Resolves YES if the winner of 2026 FIFA World Cup is Brazil, Germany, Italy, Argentina, France, Uruguay, Spain, or England.
reference_class: World Cup tournaments where the semifinal field is known
reasoning: As of 2026-07-12, the World Cup quarterfinals are complete and the semifinal lineup is confirmed: France, Spain, England, and Argentina — all four ranked top-4 in FIFA rankings. Every one of these four is on the qualifying list of past champions (Argentina 1978/86/2022, France 1998/2018, England 1966, Spain 2010), so whichever team wins the final on 2026-07-19, the question resolves YES. This is no longer a probabilistic forecast in the ordinary sense; it is close to a resolved fact pending only the tournament actually being played out. Reference class: in essentially all past World Cups, the final-four have included at least one former champion (base rate for that alone is very high, ~90%+ historically), but here it's stronger — literally all 4 teams qualify. Residual uncertainty is only tournament-completion risk (extreme weather, security incident, forfeiture) which searches show is not currently threatening the semifinal/final fixtures despite unrelated political controversies (travel bans on Haiti/Iran, a disputed red-card reversal for Belgium) that don't involve these 4 teams.
what_would_change_my_mind: A semifinal or final match is abandoned/forfeited in a way that lets FIFA declare a walkover result
A major security incident or force majeure event cancels the remainder of the tournament
Evidence that one of the 4 semifinalists faces disqualification (doping, eligibility fraud) before the final

question: Perfect score achieved by an AI model in the International Math Olympiad (IMO) 2026?
resolution_criterion:
same settings as this, but for a perfect score, not gold, and for the International Math Olympiad (IMO) 2026, not 2025.

(https://manifold.markets/embed/jack/will-an-ai-win-a-gold-medal-on-imo)
reference_class: Year-over-year AI performance progression at IMO (2024 silver, 2025 gold missing hardest problem) combined with base rate of perfect scores among elite human IMO contestants (~1%)
reasoning: AI went from silver (2024, 4/6 solved) to gold (2025, 5/6 solved, missing only the deliberately brutal P6 that stumped all but 6/630 humans). A naive trend extrapolation suggests a full sweep is plausible in 2026, and 2026 capability signals (GPT-5.4 at 95% on USAMO 2026) show continued rapid progress, with multiple labs (OpenAI, DeepMind, others) likely attempting again this year, raising the 'at least one succeeds' odds. But IMO's hardest problem slot is specifically engineered to resist systematic/creative-shortcut approaches each year, is not guaranteed to be easier in 2026, and even the best humans achieve perfect scores at under 1% base rate. Balancing the encouraging trend and capability gains against the structural difficulty wall on the hardest problem(s), I land moderately below even odds.
what_would_change_my_mind: Official/credible pre-competition or early leaks indicating a lab specifically engineered its 2026 system to target creative combinatorics/geometry weaknesses that caused the 2025 P6 failure
Reports that IMO 2026's hardest problem is unusually approachable relative to typical P3/P6 difficulty
Multiple independent labs converging on full 42/42 within days of the July 15-16 papers, suggesting the barrier has genuinely fallen rather than one lucky problem set
Evidence that reasoning models now handle deep single-insight combinatorial constructions (the classic AI failure mode) markedly better than in 2025 benchmarks

question: Perfect score achieved by an AI model in the International Math Olympiad (IMO) 2026?
resolution_criterion:
same settings as this, but for a perfect score, not gold, and for the International Math Olympiad (IMO) 2026, not 2025.

(https://manifold.markets/embed/jack/will-an-ai-win-a-gold-medal-on-imo)
reference_class: AI systems' cumulative IMO problems solved per year (2024: 4/6, 2025: 5/6) and rate of new 'first-ever' AI milestones being claimed within the same year they become plausible
reasoning: IMO 2026 contest days are July 15-16, 2026 (Shanghai) — the event has not yet occurred as of the forecast timestamp; no results exist yet. Base rate/trend: AI went from 4/6 problems solved (2024, silver) to 5/6 (2025, gold) across two major general models (OpenAI experimental, Gemini Deep Think), with a formal system (ByteDance Seed Prover) also nearing 5/6 with extended compute. Both years, one specifically hard problem (P3/P6-style) resisted every system, so a genuine 'perfect' first has never happened. Multiple well-funded labs (OpenAI, Google DeepMind, ByteDance, likely additional Chinese entrants given the Shanghai host) will independently attempt IMO 2026, and resolution criteria mirror the looser, self-reported 'gold medal' precedent (not official IMO certification), which lowers the practical bar and allows extended-compute formal verification. This raises P(at least one success) above any single lab's individual chance. Landed at 0.81: slightly below the market's 0.85, preserving weight on the 'first-ever full clear' tail risk given only two fragile trend data points and the specific design intent of IMO's hardest problems to resist systematic/creative brute force.
what_would_change_my_mind: Official/credible leak of IMO 2026 problem statements showing unusually tractable P3/P6 difficulty
Pre-contest announcements of new dedicated math-proving systems (successor to AlphaProof/Seed Prover) with stronger benchmark results
Early contest-day reports of which problems stumped human contestants, which correlates with AI difficulty
Post-contest claims (or absence of claims) from OpenAI/DeepMind/ByteDance within resolve-by window

question: Strait of Hormuz traffic returns to normal by end of July? [Polymarket]
resolution_criterion:
Resolves as Strait of Hormuz traffic returns to normal by July 31? 

[link preview]
reference_class: War-driven closures of major maritime chokepoints (e.g., Suez 1967-75, Bab-el-Mandeb 2023-24 Houthi disruption) resolving to full pre-crisis traffic within 3 weeks of a ceasefire breakdown
reasoning: Active war resumed July 8 after ceasefire collapse; Iran attacked commercial ships days earlier and traffic has fallen to ~4-8% of pre-crisis levels (5-11 vessels/day vs 120-140 normal). US officials estimate mine clearance could take up to 6 months; insurers project Hormuz war-risk downgrade not before Q4 2026, full normalization Q1-Q2 2027. Even the brief June 17 memorandum period did not achieve full normal traffic before fighting resumed. With only 19 days to July 31 and active hostilities ongoing, structural (mines, insurance) and diplomatic (broken ceasefire) barriers make 'return to normal' by month-end extremely unlikely.
what_would_change_my_mind: A durable, verified ceasefire with Iran publicly declaring the strait safe and no further attacks for several days
Confirmed completion of mine-sweeping across the transit corridor
Major War Risk Committee downgrading Hormuz's listed-area status ahead of the Q4 2026 estimate
AIS-tracked daily transits rapidly climbing back toward 100+ vessels/day well before July 31

question: Strait of Hormuz traffic returns to normal by end of July? [Polymarket]
resolution_criterion:
Resolves as Strait of Hormuz traffic returns to normal by July 31? 

[link preview]
reference_class: Conflict-driven maritime chokepoint closures (Red Sea/Bab-el-Mandeb Houthi disruption, 1980s Tanker War Hormuz mining) and their post-ceasefire traffic recovery timelines
reasoning: Active US-Iran war has driven Hormuz traffic to near-zero (11-14 vessels/day vs ~88 normal). A June 17 MOU briefly allowed partial recovery but even then only reached ~20% of normal traffic before collapsing on June 20. As of today (July 12), IRGC has re-declared the strait closed after fresh strikes on commercial vessels and renewed US-Iran combat. Reference class of conflict-driven chokepoint closures (Red Sea/Houthi disruption, historical Tanker War mining) shows full normalization essentially never happens within ~19 days of active fighting, even after ceasefires. Market at 3.4% appears informed (45 bettors tracking a well-covered live war) and I weight close to it.
what_would_change_my_mind: A verified durable US-Iran-Israel ceasefire holding for multiple weeks with no new strikes
P&I insurers restoring standard Gulf war-risk coverage
Daily transit counts climbing back toward 80+/day before July 31
Any further tanker strikes or mine incidents, which would confirm continued disruption

question: Title of the movie spoken within "The Odyssey"?
resolution_criterion:
Resolves YES if the word "odyssey" is said at any point during Christopher Nolan's upcoming "The Odyssey" movie. Resolves NO otherwise. 

What matters is the word "odyssey". The full title of the movie is generally stylized as "The Odyssey", but I'm ignoring the leading "The" for the purposes of this question. "An odyssey", "the odyssey", or etc would all count. 

The word needs to be spoken within the film (not just shown on screen). 

The name "Odysseus" does not count—that's a related name, not the word itself.

Other forms of the same word like the plural "odysseys" would count. 

hat tip to the retired @mattyb for the idea.
reference_class: Films titled after a common English noun/word that also describes their own plot (self-referential titles), rate at which the title word is spoken as dialogue/narration
reasoning: Reference class of movies titled after a common noun describing their own plot (not a proper name) shows high title-drop rates, and Nolan personally has a strong pattern of explicit title drops (Tenet, Dunkirk, Inception all speak their titles as key dialogue). The film's bardic/oral-poetry narration framing (Travis Scott's character telling the tale) creates a natural vehicle for the word 'odyssey' as a common noun meaning 'long journey.' Countervailing: the one confirmed marketing line ('A war, a man, a trick') is terse and modern, and Nolan emphasized avoiding archaic-sounding dialogue, slightly tempering confidence. No review or leaked transcript directly confirms or denies the word being spoken.
what_would_change_my_mind: A confirmed transcript or detailed review explicitly stating the word is/isn't said
Nolan or cast interview explicitly confirming a title-drop line
Full script leak or post-release Reddit/critic consensus after wide release on July 17

question: Title of the movie spoken within "The Odyssey"?
resolution_criterion:
Resolves YES if the word "odyssey" is said at any point during Christopher Nolan's upcoming "The Odyssey" movie. Resolves NO otherwise. 

What matters is the word "odyssey". The full title of the movie is generally stylized as "The Odyssey", but I'm ignoring the leading "The" for the purposes of this question. "An odyssey", "the odyssey", or etc would all count. 

The word needs to be spoken within the film (not just shown on screen). 

The name "Odysseus" does not count—that's a related name, not the word itself.

Other forms of the same word like the plural "odysseys" would count. 

hat tip to the retired @mattyb for the idea.
reference_class: single-word abstract-noun movie titles where the title word is spoken as dialogue (not a character name)
reasoning: Nolan chose deliberately plain/modern/earthy dialogue over literary language, and every trailer transcript found uses 'Odysseus' repeatedly but never the common noun 'odyssey.' The word 'odyssey' as an abstract common noun is a post-hoc derivation from the poem's title and doesn't appear self-referentially in the source material, making its use in-scene feel anachronistic/meta — something Nolan's stated approach seems to avoid. Critics have already seen the full film (press/premiere screenings in early July) and intensely scrutinized its dialogue (the viral 'daddy' line), yet no coverage found flags an odyssey title-drop moment. Market (10%, 49 bettors, real volume) tracks my independent reasoning closely with no sign of private information, so I read it as informed and land near it.
what_would_change_my_mind: A confirmed reviewer/audience report quoting a line using 'odyssey' or 'odysseys'
A leaked script excerpt showing narrator or character use of the word
Full public release reactions (starting July 17) flagging the line either way

question: Will the next full gemini model be as good as opus 4.7 or gpt 5.5 at coding?
resolution_criterion: resolves to my impression a week or two after launch and I've had the opportunity to try it out. Flash/instant/mini doesn't count. Should be pro or similar. 3.2 pro-preview, 3.5 pro, 4 pro all okay
reference_class: New flagship LLM releases (major lab) judged by an engaged technical evaluator against the current top 1-2 coding models at time of release
reasoning: Reference class: new flagship LLM releases matching the then-current top 2 coding models at launch, judged by an engaged evaluator — historically ~40-50% base rate, with the current leader's tooling/ecosystem edge tending to persist a cycle. This cycle has specific negative signals: Gemini 3.5 Pro delayed twice (June->July 17, 2026) reportedly due to coding/reasoning quality gaps versus flagship bar, plus real organizational turmoil at Google DeepMind (Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer departed for OpenAI, June 2026, corroborated by Axios/CNBC/Yahoo Finance). Current Gemini 3.1 Pro already trails Opus 4.7/GPT-5.5 on agentic coding benchmarks (SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal-Bench) in most credible sources, though closer on plain SWE-Bench Verified. Countervailing: Google has resources to close gaps fast, and 'as good as' is a parity bar, not a 'better than' bar, and the creator accepts several possible next-model names (3.2/3.5/4 Pro), giving room. Timing risk: market closes July 19, just ~2 days after a possible July 17 launch, leaving too little time for the required 'week or two' hands-on impression, which favors NO or non-resolution on the stated date.
what_would_change_my_mind: Gemini 3.5/4 Pro launches and independent evaluators (SWE-bench, Terminal-Bench, Aider leaderboard) show it beating or matching Opus 4.7/GPT-5.5 on agentic coding tasks
Reports emerge that the coding-quality concerns cited in the delay were resolved before GA
Further high-profile DeepMind departures or continued delays past July, suggesting persistent quality/organizational problems
The market creator publishes early hands-on impressions post-launch that lean clearly positive or negative on coding specifically

question: Will the next full gemini model be as good as opus 4.7 or gpt 5.5 at coding?
resolution_criterion: resolves to my impression a week or two after launch and I've had the opportunity to try it out. Flash/instant/mini doesn't count. Should be pro or similar. 3.2 pro-preview, 3.5 pro, 4 pro all okay
reference_class: Major-lab (Google/OpenAI/Anthropic) flagship LLM launches judged by informed observers within 1-2 weeks of GA as coding-competitive with the current best rival model(s)
reasoning: Gemini 3.5 Pro (the likely 'next full' model) hasn't reached GA as of 2026-07-12; Google explicitly delayed it from June to July citing coding/reasoning quality not yet flagship-ready, a direct bearish signal on this exact question. Countervailing: the resolution bar is disjunctive (as good as Opus 4.7 OR GPT-5.5) and subjective/impressionistic, which is easier to clear than topping SWE-bench; Google's Gemini 3-line has generally landed frontier-competitive at launch historically. Reported (noisy) benchmarks show current Gemini tiers trailing Opus 4.7 clearly and running close to but under GPT-5.5 on coding. Team turmoil (reported departures) adds uncertainty. Market at 0.72 looks like an informed, actively-engaged crowd already pricing the delay news; I land slightly below it given how squarely the delay rationale targets coding quality specifically.
what_would_change_my_mind: Gemini 3.5 Pro GA ships with published SWE-bench/Terminal-Bench scores matching or beating GPT-5.5
Independent early-tester reviews (not Google marketing) call coding quality on par with Opus/GPT-5.5
GA slips again past the question's effective resolution window, likely reducing chance of a clear favorable impression forming in time
Market price moves sharply on release-day benchmark leaks

question: Gemini perfect score on IMO 2026?
resolution_criterion:
This year, 2025 gemini got a 35, a gold medal, but will it ever get a 42 (on IMO 2026)???

Update 2025-08-31 (PST) (AI summary of creator comment): - Applies to: The Gemini model that actually competes in the 2026 IMO (whatever the new Gemini version entered is).

YES if that specific model scores a perfect 42/42. NO otherwise.
reference_class: AI models' year-over-year IMO score trajectory (silver 2024 to gold 2025) combined with human perfect-scorer rarity (5/630 in 2025)
reasoning: IMO 2026 contest (July 15-16) hasn't occurred yet as of this run (2026-07-12), so this is pure forward extrapolation. Gemini Deep Think scored 35/42 gold at IMO 2025 but got 0 on the hardest problem (P6), a documented weak spot on maximally creative problems. Only 5/630 human contestants scored a perfect 42/42 in 2025, showing how rare a clean sweep is even for elite humans. Gemini 3 Deep Think (Feb 2026) shows large further reasoning gains, supporting continued improvement, but the last mile to zero-error-across-6-problems is qualitatively harder than broad benchmark gains, and competing labs (OpenAI, ByteDance/Lean-based systems) could reach a perfect score first instead of Gemini. Landed below the 'any AI perfect score' base rate because the question requires it be Gemini specifically.
what_would_change_my_mind: Official pre-contest or leaked reports of a much stronger Gemini Deep Think variant specifically tuned on hardest-problem-style combinatorics/number theory
News that DeepMind is fielding a hybrid formal-verification + LLM system (akin to AlphaProof) for IMO 2026 rather than pure natural-language Deep Think
Independent evaluations shortly before the contest showing near-saturated performance on 2025-style P3/P6 problems

question: Gemini perfect score on IMO 2026?
resolution_criterion:
This year, 2025 gemini got a 35, a gold medal, but will it ever get a 42 (on IMO 2026)???

Update 2025-08-31 (PST) (AI summary of creator comment): - Applies to: The Gemini model that actually competes in the 2026 IMO (whatever the new Gemini version entered is).

YES if that specific model scores a perfect 42/42. NO otherwise.
reference_class: AI systems' year-over-year IMO score trajectory (28/42 in 2024 -> 35/42 in 2025) and the historical rate at which IMO gold medalists achieve a literal 42/42 perfect score
reasoning: Base rate: AI IMO score jumped 28->35/42 in one year (2024->2025), but marginal gains compress near a ceiling, and a literal 42/42 requires flawless P1-P5 execution PLUS solving P3 and P6 -- problems specifically designed to resist systematic/pattern-matching approaches, historically solved by <10% of human gold medalists. Fresh USAMO 2026 data shows Gemini-3.1-Pro trailing GPT-5.4 badly on hardest proof problems (74.4% vs 95.2% near-saturation), suggesting Gemini's frontier checkpoint hasn't yet closed the gap on exactly this kind of problem, though the actual IMO entrant will be a specialized high-compute system (like last year's Deep Think), not the generic Pro model. Countervailing: Google has strong reputational incentive to one-up 2025's gold-medal result given OpenAI's parallel success, and Deep Think-style extensive test-time search could plausibly crack even P6 given enough compute. Market sits at 47% but I read it as thin (163 bettors, moderate volume, play money, visible informed pushback from a trader who argued fair value is ~38%) rather than deeply informed, so I lean below it toward my own aggregate.
what_would_change_my_mind: Official confirmation Gemini enters IMO 2026 under the same natural-language, time-limited protocol as 2025
Any leaked/reported performance on 2026 problems or a comparable hard olympiad (post-USAMO-2026) showing near-saturation on P3/P6-style problems
News that DeepMind specifically re-tuned Deep Think for combinatorics/number-theory 'killer' problems ahead of this IMO
A sharp, sustained move in the Manifold price with high volume from apparently well-informed traders

question: Will Norway Win The 2026 FIFA World Cup?
resolution_criterion:
Resolution Criteria

The 2026 FIFA World Cup takes place from June 11 to July 19, 2026. This market resolves YES if Norway wins the tournament. Resolution will be determined by the official FIFA World Cup 2026 final results, available at https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026.

Background

Norway qualified directly for the World Cup finals as group winners in UEFA qualifying. The 2026 FIFA World Cup expanded to 48 teams, with teams split into twelve groups of four teams, with the top two teams in each group and the eight best third-placed teams progressing to a new round of 32. The tournament will be jointly hosted by sixteen cities—eleven in the United States, three in Mexico, and two in Canada.

Considerations

Norway has not won a FIFA World Cup in its history. The tournament's expanded 48-team format means more teams advance from the group stage, potentially creating a more unpredictable knockout phase. Norway is drawn in Group I alongside France, Senegal, and a UEFA playoff winner.

This description was generated by AI.
reference_class: Teams eliminated in the World Cup quarterfinals
reasoning: Already-resolved check confirms Norway is eliminated: they finished Group I runners-up behind France, beat Côte d'Ivoire (R32) and Brazil 2-1 (R16), then lost 2-1 to England in extra time in the quarterfinals on July 11, 2026. Semifinalists are France, Spain, England, and Argentina. With Norway out of the tournament, they cannot win the 2026 World Cup; residual probability reflects only the negligible chance the market resolves on a technicality rather than actual on-field outcome.
what_would_change_my_mind: Evidence the quarterfinal result was overturned or annulled by FIFA
A market-resolution technicality unrelated to on-field results

question: Will Norway Win The 2026 FIFA World Cup?
resolution_criterion:
Resolution Criteria

The 2026 FIFA World Cup takes place from June 11 to July 19, 2026. This market resolves YES if Norway wins the tournament. Resolution will be determined by the official FIFA World Cup 2026 final results, available at https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026.

Background

Norway qualified directly for the World Cup finals as group winners in UEFA qualifying. The 2026 FIFA World Cup expanded to 48 teams, with teams split into twelve groups of four teams, with the top two teams in each group and the eight best third-placed teams progressing to a new round of 32. The tournament will be jointly hosted by sixteen cities—eleven in the United States, three in Mexico, and two in Canada.

Considerations

Norway has not won a FIFA World Cup in its history. The tournament's expanded 48-team format means more teams advance from the group stage, potentially creating a more unpredictable knockout phase. Norway is drawn in Group I alongside France, Senegal, and a UEFA playoff winner.

This description was generated by AI.
reference_class: Eliminated teams in single-elimination knockout tournaments winning the tournament
reasoning: Already-resolved check (mandatory first step) found the decisive fact: Norway was eliminated from the 2026 World Cup, losing 2-1 to England in extra time in the quarterfinal on July 11, 2026 (Bellingham scored twice; Norway led via Schjelderup). This is corroborated by England FA's own match centre, CBS Sports, Al Jazeera, and ESPN/Yahoo coverage of the bracket. Norway topped Group I (beat Senegal 3-2, lost to France 1-4) and advanced through the round of 32/16, but the quarterfinal loss ends any path to winning the tournament — a team cannot win a single-elimination competition after elimination. Reference class: probability an eliminated knockout-tournament team wins the tournament = 0 by construction. Base rate before this evidence (pre-tournament reference class of a good-but-not-elite European nation with a star player) was already low (~2-4%); post-elimination it collapses to the residual floor for source/reporting error only.
what_would_change_my_mind: Credible evidence that the reported quarterfinal result is incorrect or that FIFA's official records show a different outcome
Any indication the match was later voided/replayed due to a formal protest with a different final outcome

question: Will Mitch McConnell make a verifiable, live public appearance before August 3, 2026?
resolution_criterion:
This market resolves YES if Mitch McConnell makes a live public appearance, or appears in clearly same-day public video, before August 3, 2026.

Deadline: August 2, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.

To count, the appearance must be independently verifiable through a source such as C-SPAN, a Senate livestream, live TV, a public Senate proceeding, or footage/reporting from a major news organization.

It does not count if the only evidence is released by McConnell’s campaign, Senate office, family, allies, or personal/social media accounts. 

Written statements, private calls, old footage, undated photos/videos, and claims that he is “working” or “recovering” without public visual evidence also do not count.

This market resolves NO if the deadline passes without a qualifying appearance.

—

Why August 3: Kentucky changed its U.S. Senate vacancy law in 2024. The governor can no longer appoint an interim senator; vacancies are filled by special election. Recent reporting suggests August 3 may be a key cutoff: a vacancy before then could trigger a separate special-election process, while a vacancy on or after then may effectively be folded into the regular November election. (The law is new and the exact process could be contested.)
reference_class: Elderly (80+) US officials hospitalized after a severe cardiac/neurological event (cardiac arrest w/ CPR, stroke), time to first independently-verifiable public appearance
reasoning: McConnell suffered an apparent cardiac arrest requiring CPR on June 14, 2026, and has been hospitalized continuously since with zero independently verifiable public appearance for 4+ weeks despite intense pressure (Beshear's public letter, death-hoax rumors, unconfirmed brain-death reports from independent journalists). Office statements are vague written releases that explicitly do not qualify under this market's criteria; third-hand claims of phone conversations (Barrasso, Scott Jennings) also don't qualify. Reference class: Fetterman's materially less severe 2022 ischemic stroke still took ~9 weeks to produce any public appearance; McConnell's cardiac-arrest event carries higher hypoxic brain injury risk and he's already 4 weeks in with only ~3 weeks left before the Aug 2 deadline. Countervailing: strong political incentive (Senate back in session, vacancy-law scrutiny, rumor control) could push his team toward a brief camera-verifiable appearance (e.g., a floor vote, which requires physical presence since the Senate has no proxy voting) if he is genuinely improving as claimed. Net: leans NO but not overwhelmingly.
what_would_change_my_mind: Any C-SPAN/Senate livestream footage of him on the floor or in a hearing
A live TV interview or press conference
Major news org same-day video/photo from a public setting
Credible on-record medical confirmation of full consciousness/capacity from an independent source (not staff/family)

—

Why August 3: Kentucky changed its U.S. Senate vacancy law in 2024. The governor can no longer appoint an interim senator; vacancies are filled by special election. Recent reporting suggests August 3 may be a key cutoff: a vacancy before then could trigger a separate special-election process, while a vacancy on or after then may effectively be folded into the regular November election. (The law is new and the exact process could be contested.)
reference_class: Octogenarian US senators hospitalized for serious/undisclosed cardiac events with prolonged (3+ week) media blackout on their condition (analogues: Feinstein 2023 shingles absence, Fetterman 2022 stroke recovery)
reasoning: McConnell (84) suffered cardiac arrest June 14, 2026, hospitalized ~4 weeks with zero verifiable public appearance despite intense press scrutiny (leaked EMS audio, ambulance footage from June 14, demands from Beshear and Trump). Office only offers vague 'continuing recovery' statements, no visual proof. Resolution bar is generous (any independently-verifiable live appearance or same-day video, including a discharge photo), and political incentives (narrow 53-47 GOP majority, Aug 3 special-election deadline, mounting transparency pressure) create real motive to produce a reassuring appearance if he's able. But a full month of total visual blackout under heavy scrutiny is a strong signal of serious ongoing incapacitation rather than mere privacy. Two independent markets (Manifold 15%, Polymarket 'votes by July 31' 18%) converge, read as informed pricing given heavy current coverage and active trading, so I anchor close to but slightly above that range given the generous appearance-only bar vs. the stricter voting bar Polymarket is pricing.
what_would_change_my_mind: Any leaked photo/video of McConnell in the hospital or being discharged
An official announcement of a discharge date or planned Senate return
Confirmation of a stroke, coma, or other severe neurological injury (would push probability down further)
A scheduled close Senate vote where GOP leadership signals they need/expect his physical presence
News of resignation or death (resolves NO)

question: Open-Source AI model gets perfect IMO 2026 score? [International Math Olympiad 2026]
resolution_criterion:
all questions right & all points received.

Usual rules:

No internet

As much allotted real-time as humans, parallel reasoning allowed

Lean4 or other theorem proving software allowed

Natural language proofs and formal proofs allowed

The model completing the task must be open-weight, but the scaffold it makes use of need not be open-source.

Note: the open-weight model must be released before the IMO 2026 to count.
reference_class: AI systems (open and closed) attempting IMO under contest conditions, 2024-2026; human perfect-scorers per IMO year
reasoning: Perfect IMO scores (42/42) are rare even for elite humans (<1% of contestants, sometimes zero per year) and no AI system — closed or open — has ever achieved one; best 2025 systems (OpenAI, Gemini Deep Think, DeepSeek Math-V2) all capped at gold-level 5/6 (35/42), not perfect. Open-weight models have closed the gold-level gap fast (no open gold pre-mid-2025 to open gold by Nov 2025), and Chinese labs have strong incentive given Shanghai hosting plus contest rules reward heavy test-time compute, but going from 'gold' to 'all points on all 6 problems' is a categorically harder bar (IMO problems are designed to include at least one problem resistant to systematic solving). No announced open model shows evidence of exceeding 5/6-level performance as of July 2026.
what_would_change_my_mind: A verified report of an open-weight model scoring 6/6 full marks on IMO 2026 or a recent mock/shortlist set under contest-equivalent conditions
A major open lab (DeepSeek, Qwen, GLM, Tencent) announcing a specifically scaled test-time-compute system targeting this exact challenge with strong pre-IMO validation
Evidence that IMO 2026's problem set skewed easier than typical years, making a clean sweep more tractable

question: Open-Source AI model gets perfect IMO 2026 score? [International Math Olympiad 2026]
resolution_criterion:
all questions right & all points received.

Usual rules:

No internet

As much allotted real-time as humans, parallel reasoning allowed

Lean4 or other theorem proving software allowed

Natural language proofs and formal proofs allowed

The model completing the task must be open-weight, but the scaffold it makes use of need not be open-source.

Note: the open-weight model must be released before the IMO 2026 to count.
reference_class: Year-over-year AI performance at IMO under contest conditions (2019-2025), and open-vs-closed-weight gap-closing on frontier math benchmarks
reasoning: IMO 2026 sits days away (exam July 15-16, 2026); no results exist yet. Trend: best AI went 4/6 (2024, closed) -> 5/6 (2025, both closed AND open-weight DeepSeekMath-V2 matched gold same year), but 6/6 (perfect) has never been achieved by any model, open or closed. The open/closed gap on math reasoning specifically has narrowed a lot, which is the main reason to be above a low base rate. Countervailing: a genuinely clean sweep including the hardest problem is a much bigger jump than 'gold' (5/6), historically achieved by only a handful of ~600+ human contestants per year; strict no-internet/human-timed/official-grading conditions raise execution risk beyond benchmark scores. Cross-checked against two related Manifold contracts: general 'any AI gets perfect score' trades ~87%, 'Gemini specifically' ~52%, and this open-weight-specific contract ~31% -- an internally coherent ordering. My independent estimates (20-37%) cluster near the market's 31% without giving me a clear informational edge over it.
what_would_change_my_mind: A credible pre-competition report that a named open-weight model (DeepSeek, Kimi, Qwen, GLM, etc.) already scored a clean 6/6 on 2025 IMO problems or an equivalently hard held-out set under contest-style conditions
News that a major open-weight lab is fielding an official, well-resourced IMO 2026 attempt (mirroring OpenAI/DeepMind's 2025 efforts)
Official IMO 2026 results (expected within days) showing any model's actual score breakdown
Evidence the market is unusually thin/concentrated among a few overconfident traders rather than broad-based
